commentaries.author: Bede
commentaries.content: blasphemed: That is, because some sins are the cause of sin, some the punishment of sin, some both; as Isaiah says, "Behold thou art wroth, and we have sinned." In this place "to blaspheme God, because of the hail," is acknowledged to be both sin, and the punishment of sin. <a

commentaries.author: Bede
commentaries.content: hail: The hail of the wrath of God is likened to a talent; for it is both heavy in weight, and equal in judgment, and is inflicted upon all according to the diversity of their faults. And all the plagues of Egypt were figures of spiritual plagues.

commentaries.author: George Leo Haydock
commentaries.content: And great hail like a talent came down Ver. 21. Et grando magna sicut talentum, os talantiaia, quasi talentaris. The Protestants and Mr. N. translate hail about the weight of a talent, as if every hailstone were of that weight.
